message.content:
Research the CURRENT (2025) version of the open-source PM tool **Plane** (GitHub: makeplane/plane, docs at docs.plane.so, site plane.so) and evaluate it against a specific company's requirements. Use web research — fetch the GitHub repo README/releases, the official docs, and recent (2024-2025) sources. Do NOT rely on any local checkout; I need the newest upstream state. Be precise, cite URLs, and be honest about gaps.

CONTEXT: A consumer-goods licensing company wants to replace ClickUp with a self-hosted PM system. Their workflow is heavily custom-data-model + workflow oriented (custom object types with many typed fields, custom multi-stage pipelines, a searchable "design library," role-based field visibility, bulk stage changes). The KEY question: they do NOT need everything built-in, as long as they can build 100% of their needed functionality ON TOP of the platform WITHOUT forking it — i.e., via a stable API, SDK, webhooks, or a plugin/extension system. So extensibility and "can I add custom objects/fields/UI without modifying core source" matters as much as built-in features.

Evaluate Plane against these requirements (mark each: Built-in / Buildable-on-top-without-fork / Requires-fork / Not-possible, with evidence):
- R1: Multiple custom OBJECT/ISSUE TYPES (they need Project, SKU, Collection, Design, Style# as distinct types with different fields).
- R2: TYPED CUSTOM FIELDS/PROPERTIES per type — text, number, date, single-select/enum, boolean, relation/link, user/member. **Critical: Did the newest Plane add "Issue Types and Custom Properties"? Is that feature in the SELF-HOSTED COMMUNITY edition, or is it gated behind Plane Pro/Business/Enterprise/Cloud? Check the source: is there an `ee` (enterprise edition) directory in makeplane/plane that gates it? What's the licensing of that code?**
- R3: Multi-level HIERARCHY — can issues nest ≥3 levels deep (sub-issues of sub-issues), or only parent→child? Are there "Epics"?
- R4: Custom WORKFLOW STATES per project/type, with STATE-TRANSITION HISTORY/timestamps available via API (to compute time-in-stage). Does Plane have workflow/transition rules now?
- R5: BULK operations via API (multi-select change state/assignee/field) — what bulk endpoints exist?
- R6: Saved filtered/grouped VIEWS.
- R7: Role-based access control — is there any FIELD-LEVEL permission (e.g., hide a "pricing" field from a "designer" role)? Or only project-level roles (Admin/Member/Guest)?
- R8: Strong cross-record SEARCH/FILTER.
- R9: File attachments via S3/R2; ability to store external thumbnail URLs + filepath links.
- R10: REST/GraphQL API + WEBHOOKS. How complete is the API? Is there an official SDK (Python/JS/TS)? Is there an "API/integrations" or app framework?
- R11: EXTENSIBILITY WITHOUT FORKING — does Plane have any PLUGIN/EXTENSION system for adding custom UI or custom backend logic? Or is the only path "fork the monorepo"? Is the frontend (Next.js) extensible without forking? Can you build a separate app against their API and embed/SSO? Note: Plane's frontend has no plugin SDK as far as I know — verify the current state.
- R12: Self-hostable; LICENSE (AGPL? what version?); what features are open vs commercial/EE-gated.
- R13: UX maturity for non-technical users.

ALSO report: project maturity (stars, release cadence, latest version, company backing, funding), and any official "developer platform / API / SDK / app marketplace" plans.

Return: (1) a requirement-by-requirement verdict table, (2) a clear bottom-line answer to "Can this company build 100% of their custom functionality on top of self-hosted Plane WITHOUT forking?", (3) the single biggest risk/blocker, (4) cited URLs.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Run the "deep-research" workflow.

Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report.

When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Phases:
- Scope: Decompose question (from args) into 5 search angles
- Search: 5 parallel WebSearch agents, one per angle
- Fetch: URL-dedup, fetch top 15 sources, extract falsifiable claims
- Verify: 3-vote adversarial verification per claim (need 2/3 refutes to kill)
- Synthesize: Merge semantic dupes, rank by confidence, cite sources

## Search Results for Plane Project Management

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Plane's API documentation, webhooks, Python SDK, and developer platform:

### API & REST Documentation

The Plane API is organized around REST with predictable resource-oriented URLs, accepts application/json request bodies, returns JSON responses, and uses standard HTTP response codes, authentication, and verbs. The API documentation is available at the official Plane developer portal.

### Webhooks

Plane supports webhooks for Project, Issue, Cycle, Module, and Issue Comment events, which can be triggered when there is a change in an event, such as when a new project is created, updated or deleted. The platform offers HMAC-signed webhooks as part of its integration capabilities.

### Python SDK

Plane provides typed SDKs in Node.js and Python, with the Python SDK available on GitHub at `makeplane/plane-python-sdk`. The SDK is designed to work with Plane's REST API.

### Developer Platform

The Plane developer platform provides everything needed to self-host Plane, integrate with the REST API, configure webhooks, and build powerful custom workflows. The platform includes REST API with OAuth 2.0 for building custom integrations, dashboards, and automations.

The official developer documentation is available at **developers.plane.so**.

Plane is a modern project management platform to manage tasks, sprints, docs, and triage.","url":"https://github.com/makeplane/plane"},{"title":"[bug]: The community edition shows error when trying to access the system · Issue #7003 · makeplane/plane","url":"https://github.com/makeplane/plane/issues/7003"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Plane (makeplane), Issue Types, Custom Properties, and the differences between self-hosted editions:

## Plane Editions Overview

Plane comes in four editions by how it's deployed, with three unique self-hosted editions: the open-source Community Edition, the recommended Commercial Edition, and the Airgapped Edition.

### Community vs. Enterprise/Commercial Edition

**Community Edition:**
- The Community Edition is completely open-source, with no restrictions beyond those outlined in the AGPL v3.0 license
- Community Edition is open source under AGPL v3.0, free with no license key and full source available, with feature parity with the Free tier of Cloud
- Unlimited projects, work items, cycles, modules, pages, five layout views, intake, dashboards, estimates, REST API, and webhooks with no user limits

**Commercial/Enterprise Edition:**
- Community Edition covers core project management, while Commercial adds governance and scale: workflows, approvals, SSO, audit trails, epics, integrations, and air-gapped deployment
- Plane differentiates through custom issue types and custom fields on Business plan

### License Information

Community is AGPL with no key, Commercial uses an online license key tied to one workspace and one machine, and Airgapped uses an offline license bundle.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Plane's features related to your query:

## Workflows and Custom States

Plane allows you to add an unlimited number of states and organize them based on your preferences. Plane provides customizable states to manage work item progress, organized through backlog, unstarted, started, completed, and cancelled state groups.

## Epics and Hierarchy

Epics help you group related tasks into a larger work item, providing a hierarchical structure for managing complex projects, ideal for breaking down major objectives into smaller, manageable pieces while keeping everything organized within a project.

## Sub-Issues

Rich text descriptions, comments, activity logs, attachments, and sub-issues provide full context on issue detail pages.

## 2025 Release Updates

Recent releases include the ability to archive epics to keep your workspace focused, rich filters for Initiatives, and custom OAuth scopes for finer control over third-party app access.

Form Intake now supports custom work types, smarter routing, and a cleaner submission flow.

The search results don't contain specific information about bulk operations features in a 2025 release. If you need more detailed information about specific bulk operations capabilities, I'd recommend checking Plane's official changelog or documentation directly.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Plane:

## Funding

Plane, a commercial open source project management start-up out of Delaware and India, has raised $4M in seed funding. The seed round was funded entirely by OSS Capital, a venture capital firm that invests in startups that create commercial open source alternatives to established proprietary products.

## GitHub Stars

Plane has 46,000+ stars and growing on GitHub, and is the most starred open source project management system on GitHub.

## Latest Version

Plane CE v1.0.0 is now more stable, secure, reliable and performant, and this release brings the new branding on all instances, marking the start of a new chapter for plane.

## Company Backing

Plane was founded in November, 2022 by brothers Vamsi Kurama and Vihar Kurama. The company has grown significantly with 93 employees as of Apr 30, 2026.

## Product Overview

Plane is an AI-native project management platform with three products in one workspace: Projects, Wiki, and AI. Plane ships a free Community Edition under AGPL-3.0 that teams self-host on Docker or Kubernetes, with a free cloud tier also available.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Plane's licensing model and feature separation between open source and commercial editions:

## License Overview

Plane ships a free Community Edition under AGPL-3.0 that teams self-host on Docker or Kubernetes. The Community Edition is completely open-source, with no restrictions beyond those outlined in the AGPL v3.0 license.

## Open Source vs. Commercial Features

**Community Edition (Open Source):**
Community Edition covers core project management. It includes unlimited projects, work items, cycles, modules, pages, five layout views, intake, dashboards, estimates, REST API, and webhooks with no user limits.

**Commercial Edition (Proprietary):**
Commercial adds governance and scale: workflows, approvals, SSO, audit trails, epics, integrations, and air-gapped deployment. The Commercial Edition remains closed-source to offer enterprise-grade features and seamless scalability for businesses.

## Feature Gating Model

Community has parity with the Free tier of Cloud, while Commercial and Airgapped get full parity with Cloud's paid plans (Pro, Business, Enterprise Grid), license-gated. The Commercial Edition includes a built-in Free tier of 12 user seats per workspace, and to unlock Pro, Business, or Enterprise Grid features, you activate a license key.

## Code Architecture

The CE codebase is public and powers everything else in Plane — including the Commercial and Air-Gapped Editions which are built on top of CE, not beside it. Unlike some open-core companies, they've adopted a clean separation to keep things simple and transparent, with no hidden code that limits modifications on the Community Edition.

Specifically: are Issue Types, Custom Properties/Fields, Workflows, Epics, SSO, approvals, audit trails, integrations in Community or Commercial? What license is the Commercial edition code? Quote the feature breakdown.

content.content:
# Feature Comparison: Community vs. Commercial Editions

The provided documentation **does not contain a detailed feature-by-feature breakdown** comparing Community and Commercial editions.

## What the document states:

**Community Edition:**
- "AGPL v3.0 license, ensuring free and open usage"
- Described as "at par with the Free tier of the Cloud edition in its feature availability"

**Commercial Edition:**
- Offers "Full feature parity with our Cloud"
- Includes "advanced work management and security features"
- Provides "12 Free user seats per workspace"

## Critical gap:

The page explicitly does **not** list whether Issue Types, Custom Properties, Workflows, Epics, SSO, approvals, audit trails, or integrations are in either edition. The document notes the Commercial Edition is "closed-source" but doesn't specify its license type beyond that distinction.

**Recommendation:** Consult Plane's detailed feature matrix on their main docs site or contact their sales team for the specific feature breakdown you need.

# Plane Repository Analysis

**License:** The repo is licensed under the [GNU Affero General Public License v3.0](https://github.com/makeplane/plane/blob/master/LICENSE.txt). This is an open-source license requiring derivative works to also be open-source.

**Enterprise/EE Directory:** The page content doesn't explicitly mention an "ee" or enterprise-edition directory structure. The visible directories shown include `.claude/skills`, `.github`, `.husky`, `.idx`, `apps`, `deployments`, `docs`, and `packages`, but no dedicated enterprise folder is referenced.

**Community vs. Enterprise:** The README doesn't differentiate between Community and Commercial/Enterprise editions. Instead, it presents a unified offering with two deployment options: Plane Cloud (managed) or self-hosted. No mention of issue types, custom properties, or workflows appears in the provided content.

**Latest Release:** Version 1.3.1 (dated May 14, 2026)

**Star Count:** 50.6k stars

The project emphasizes openness rather than tiered licensing models—users can choose their deployment method but work with the same core platform.

# Plane Edition Feature Comparison

Based on the webpage content, here are the feature differences:

## Community Edition (AGPL-3.0 - Free/Self-Hosted)
**Included features:**
- "Unlimited projects, work items, cycles, modules, pages, five layout views, intake, dashboards, estimates, REST API, and webhooks"
- No user limits
- Projects and work items with descriptions, attachments, priorities, labels, custom states
- Pages and documentation
- Command K navigation
- Docker Compose and Kubernetes deployment

## Commercial Edition (License-Gated)
**Additional enterprise features:**
- Workflows and approval gates
- Audit trails and compliance logs
- SSO with SAML, OIDC, and LDAP
- Epics, initiatives, and teamspaces
- Work item types and custom properties
- GitHub, GitLab, and Slack integrations
- Wiki for org-wide knowledge
- Priority support and SLAs
- Air-gapped deployment support

## Key Distinction
The webpage explicitly states: "Same architecture, same UI" between editions. Community Edition handles core project management, while Commercial adds governance, compliance, and enterprise-grade integrations. Pricing for Commercial starts at $7 per seat monthly.

content.content:
# Plane Release Summary

## Latest 5 Releases

| Version | Date |
|---------|------|
| v1.3.1 | May 14, 2025 |
| v1.3.0 | April 6, 2025 |
| v1.2.3 | March 5, 2025 |
| v1.2.2 | February 23, 2025 |
| v1.2.1 | December 12, 2024 |

## Release Cadence

The project maintains approximately monthly release intervals, with security patches released as needed between major versions.

## Feature References in Release Notes

**Issue/Work Item Management:**
- v1.3.0 mentions "work item detail, list layout, sidebar, and comment UI" improvements
- v1.2.0 references "create work items, cycles, modules" via keyboard shortcuts

**Workflows & Customization:**
- v1.3.0 notes "Added timezone selection to workspace settings"
- v1.2.0 introduced "Power K" keyboard navigation for "almost everything"

**Custom Properties:**
- v1.1.0 added "filter and sort options for team members by role"
- v1.3.0 enhanced "work item detail properties UI"

The notes focus primarily on UI/UX enhancements, security fixes, and API improvements rather than detailing custom property frameworks specifically.

# Plane REST API Overview

## Resource Coverage

The Plane API covers extensive project management resources, including:

- **Core Work Items**: Creation, listing, retrieval, updates, deletion, and advanced search capabilities
- **Organization**: Projects, workspaces, teamspaces, members, and invitations
- **Categorization**: States, labels, work item types, custom properties, and initiatives
- **Planning**: Cycles, modules, epics, and milestones
- **Relations**: Links between work items, page associations, and customer linkages
- **Supporting Features**: Comments, activity logs, attachments, time tracking (worklogs), and assets
- **Business Elements**: Customers, customer properties, customer requests, and intake issues
- **Collaboration**: Pages (workspace and project-level) and stickies

## Authentication Methods

The API supports two authentication approaches: "API keys included in the `X-API-Key` header" and OAuth tokens passed as Bearer tokens in the Authorization header.

## Notable Features

- **Cursor-based pagination** with configurable per-page limits (max 100)
- **Rate limiting** at 60 requests per minute per API key
- **Selective field retrieval** via `fields` parameter and **nested data expansion** through `expand` parameter
- Exposes issue types, custom properties/values, and activity tracking

## Gaps

The documentation provided doesn't mention bulk operations, an OpenAPI specification, or official SDKs for Python or Node.js.

# Plane's Workflows Feature

## State Transition Control

Yes, Workflows control state transitions with rules. The feature enables you to "define which transitions are allowed, who can make them, and whether certain moves need approval before they take effect."

Workflows consist of defined flows between states. Members can only move items along transitions you've configured; unauthorized state changes display a blocker message explaining the restriction.

## Transition History & Timestamps

The documentation mentions a "View change history" option that "see[s] a log of all modifications made to the workflow, including who made each change and when." However, this tracks workflow configuration changes, not individual work item state transition history or timestamps.

## Commercial Availability

This is a **paid/commercial feature**. The documentation explicitly marks it as "Business" at the top and notes that approval flows are "Enterprise Grid" only.

The plan-gating language states: "Every project has a single default workflow that applies to all work items regardless of type. On the Enterprise Grid, you can create additional workflows, each scoped to specific work item types."

This indicates the default workflow is available in the Business tier, while custom type-specific workflows require Enterprise Grid.

content.content:
# Plane README Analysis

## Community vs. Commercial Features
The README makes **no distinction** between Community Edition and commercial features. There is no mention of an enterprise edition, an `ee` directory, license-gating, or feature restrictions.

## Deployment & Licensing
The document states: **"GNU Affero General Public License v3.0"** as the license. Two deployment options are offered:
- Plane Cloud (managed service)
- Self-hosted deployment via Docker or Kubernetes

## Advertised Features
The README highlights six main capabilities:

1. **Work Items** — Task creation with rich text editing and file uploads
2. **Cycles** — Sprint tracking with burn-down charts
3. **Modules** — Project segmentation into manageable units
4. **Views** — Customizable filters and saved view sharing
5. **Pages** — Note-taking with AI capabilities and rich formatting
6. **Analytics** — Real-time project insights and trend visualization

## Key Finding
The README presents Plane as a unified open-source project management tool with no apparent licensing tiers or feature gatekeeping mentioned.

# Plane Webhooks: Supported Events and Integration Features

## Webhook Events Supported

According to the documentation, Plane currently supports webhooks for these five event types:

- Project
- Issue
- Cycle
- Module
- Issue Comment

## How Webhooks Work

"A webhook triggers a HTTP POST request on the specified url, whenever there is a change in an event." The system sends different payloads depending on the action type—DELETE requests include only the entity ID, while POST and PATCH requests include the complete payload.

## OAuth and App Framework

Yes, Plane offers OAuth application support. The developer documentation includes sections on "Create an OAuth application" and "Choose token Flow," indicating a framework exists for building integrations with various authentication options.

## SDK Support

The documentation references SDKs as an available resource for developers building on Plane, though specific SDK details aren't elaborated in this excerpt.

## Community Edition Availability

The provided documentation doesn't explicitly address whether webhooks are available in the Community Edition specifically.

I found relevant search results about Plane's bulk update functionality for work items. Here's what the results show:

## Bulk Update Work Items

You can select work items by clicking the checkbox next to each one, or use the select all checkbox at the top of the list, and after selecting work items, you can update the available properties at the bottom of the screen.

Bulk operations are available in List or Table layout - bulk operations aren't available in other layouts.

## API Endpoint for Work Items

For the API side, all fields from the create endpoint are supported for updates, and you should only include fields you want to update. The API uses a PATCH method to update individual work items with supported properties like state and priority.

## Multi-Select Issues

You can select multiple items in spreadsheet view and change their type in one action.

## Security Note

It's worth noting that there have been security issues identified with the bulk update endpoints in the past. A bulk update date endpoint was found to have an IDOR vulnerability where it could fetch and modify issues from any workspace/project without proper boundary checks.

For more detailed API documentation and the specific endpoint for bulk operations, you may want to consult the official Plane API documentation at developers.plane.so.

content.content:
# Work Item Types and Custom Properties API Overview

Based on the documentation provided, I can offer limited information, as the page content focuses primarily on the update endpoint rather than comprehensive API details.

## Work Item Types API

The API supports updating work item types via a PATCH endpoint. Updatable fields include:
- Name and description
- Epic classification (`is_epic`)
- Active status (`is_active`)
- External source tracking (`external_source`, `external_id`)

The response shows the type includes properties like `logo_props`, `is_default`, and `level`.

## Custom Properties API

The sidebar navigation reveals a "Custom Properties" section with endpoints for:
- Adding, listing, and retrieving properties
- Updating and deleting properties
- Managing property values and dropdown options

However, **the provided content does not specify which property types are supported** (relation, enum, member, datetime, etc.).

## Access & Pricing

The documentation mentions a required scope: `projects.work_item_types:write`. However, **no information about plan-level gating, Business/Enterprise restrictions, or general REST API availability is present in the provided page content**.

For comprehensive details on supported property types and access restrictions, you would need to consult additional API documentation pages or contact Plane support directly.

# Plane Python SDK Overview

**Official Status**: Yes, this is the official Python SDK from makeplane (the organization behind Plane).

**Auto-Generated**: No. This is a hand-crafted SDK, not generated from OpenAPI specs. The README explicitly notes it "replaces the v0.1.x OpenAPI-generated client" with "intentional breaking changes for a cleaner, type-safe developer experience."

**Coverage**: The SDK provides comprehensive support for Plane's project management API, including:
- Work items (issues), projects, cycles, and modules
- States, labels, and custom properties
- Comments, attachments, and work logs
- Users and workspaces
- Epics, intake management, and pages

**Last Updated**: Version 0.2.15 was released on June 9, 2026 (24 total releases available).

**Key Features**:
> "Full type annotations with Pydantic v2 models" and "Built-in retry mechanism with configurable backoff"

**TypeScript/Node SDK**: The webpage does not mention a corresponding TypeScript or Node.js SDK. Only the Python implementation is documented here.

Based on the search results, here's information about Plane's roles, permissions, and access control system:

## Role Structure

Plane uses three role types - Admin, Member, and Guest - each designed for different levels of involvement and responsibility.

### Workspace-Level Roles

- **Admin**: Admins control the workspace itself - settings, members, features, and access. They determine what capabilities are available to all Members.

- **Member**: Members can do their work - create projects, manage work items, collaborate with the team - without needing administrative overhead. They see the full workspace membership and can discover public projects, but cannot alter workspace structure or invite others.

- **Guest**: Guests exist outside the workspace's core structure. They cannot see workspace members, settings, or the broader project landscape.

### Project-Level Roles

At the project level, Plane uses different roles: Admin provides full project access including settings, member management, and all features. Contributor can create and manage work items, cycles, modules, pages, and views. Commenter can view all work items in the project and add comments and reactions. Guest has limited access and can submit intake forms and view, edit, and delete intake issues they created.

## Access Control Features

Workspace Owners and Admins have full access to all teamspace content without being teamspace members — they effectively hold wildcard grants on all teamspace resources.

The search results did not contain specific information about custom roles, field-level visibility controls, or RBAC implementation details in Plane beyond the standard role hierarchy shown above.

content.content:
# Plane v1.3.1 Release Summary

**Version & Date:** v1.3.1, released May 14

**Headline Features:**

The release introduces several key improvements:

1. **User Interface Enhancement:** "Scrollbar in keyboard shortcuts modal" improves navigation within the shortcuts interface.

2. **Self-Hosted Optimization:** The system now "Skip role & use-case steps for self-hosted instances," streamlining the setup process for on-premises deployments.

3. **Performance Gains:** "Optimize sub-issue query performance" through enhanced annotations and subqueries for faster data retrieval.

4. **Platform Updates:** Removed legacy Intercom integration and updated branding from Twitter to X.

The release also includes six significant security patches addressing ORM injection vulnerabilities, workspace membership enforcement, filename sanitization, webhook SSRF protection, and configuration handling.

# Plane Permissions: Feature-Level Only

Based on the documentation, Plane's permissions matrix operates **exclusively at the feature/action level** — there is **no field-level or property-level visibility control**.

## Permission Structure

Permissions are organized by:
- **Workspace roles** (Owner, Admin, Member, Guest)
- **Project roles** (Admin, Contributor, Commenter, Guest)
- **Teamspace roles** (Member, Lead)

## What Roles Control

Each role determines whether a user can perform specific actions, such as:
- Create/edit/delete work items
- Modify properties (state, priority, assignees, labels, dates)
- Access cycles, modules, and pages
- Manage comments and attachments

The matrix shows "✓ - Permitted," "Own - creator only," or "— - Not permitted" for each action, but there's no mechanism to hide individual fields from specific roles.

For example, a Contributor can either edit work item properties or cannot — the system doesn't selectively hide certain fields (like priority or due date) from their view.

# Plane Edition Architecture

Based on the provided content, I cannot determine the specifics of how Plane's Commercial/Enterprise editions relate to the Community Edition codebase.

## What the Article States

The page indicates that all editions "share the same core" and that the Community Edition has "99.9% parity with the Free plan of our Cloud and Commercial editions." However, **the article does not explain**:

- Whether Commercial/Enterprise code is in the public `makeplane/plane` repository or closed-source
- If an enterprise edition (ee) directory exists
- How license-gated features are technically delivered (shared Docker images with keys vs. separate code)
- Which specific features require a license key

## Where to Find Answers

The article references "[technical details at docs.plane.so](https://docs.plane.so/)" and mentions a "[proprietary command-line interface](https://developers.plane.so/self-hosting/manage/prime-cli)" for Commercial Edition installation, suggesting technical documentation exists elsewhere that would address these architectural questions.

To understand the codebase structure and feature-gating approach, you would need to consult those developer resources directly.

# Plane 2025 Review: UX Maturity, Strengths/Weaknesses & Pricing

## UX Maturity for Non-Technical Users

Plane is **not designed for non-technical users**. The review explicitly states: "Like Linear, Plane serves software development teams exclusively. The interface uses development terminology (issues, sprints, backlogs)." The tool is optimized specifically for engineering workflows, making it unsuitable for marketing, sales, operations, or business teams.

## Key Strengths

- **Modern, fast interface** rivaling Linear's speed with instant page loads and smooth interactions
- **Free cloud with unlimited members**—unprecedented value in the PM category
- **Self-hosting capability** with complete data sovereignty using AGPL v3 open-source licensing
- **Cycles and Modules structure** providing dual organization (sprint-based + feature-based tracking)
- **Rapid development velocity** with weekly feature releases driven by active community

## Key Weaknesses

- **Young platform** (founded 2022) lacks battle-tested enterprise maturity
- **Smaller integration ecosystem** compared to Jira's 3,000+ marketplace apps
- **Enterprise governance still developing**—SOC 2 certification in progress, HIPAA unavailable
- **No native time tracking** functionality
- **Development-focused only**, unsuitable for broader business use cases

## Pricing Tiers

| Plan | Cost | Key Features |
|------|------|--------------|
| **Free** | $0 | Unlimited members/projects, core issue tracking, Cycles, Modules, multiple views |
| **Pro** | $7/user/month | Custom properties, advanced analytics, Pages & wiki, time tracking, AI credits |
| **Business** | $17/user/month | SSO, audit logs, approval processes, advanced permissions, intake forms |
| **Enterprise** | Custom | Dedicated support, SCIM provisioning, custom integrations |

## Custom Fields & Workflows

The review indicates that "custom properties" and "advanced workflows" are **paid features** exclusive to Pro and Business tiers respectively, representing a key distinction from the free offering.

# Prime CLI Overview

**What is it?**
The Prime CLI is a command-line management tool for Plane instances that handles "installs, upgrades, and general management" tasks, providing operators like `start`, `stop`, `restart`, and `monitor`.

**Edition availability:**
Yes, Prime CLI is exclusively for the Commercial Edition. The documentation explicitly states: "Prime CLI Commercial Edition Docker" and notes it only works with Docker installations originally set up using prime-cli.

**Commercial vs. Community delivery:**
The documentation suggests a separation between editions. There's a distinct "setup.sh script • Community Edition" section separate from Prime CLI, indicating different tooling/features by edition tier.

**Prime CLI proprietary status:**
The page doesn't explicitly state whether Prime CLI itself is proprietary code. However, the documentation structure—with Prime CLI listed as "Commercial Edition" while Community Edition uses setup.sh—implies different tools/capabilities per edition, but the open-source status of Prime CLI specifically isn't disclosed here.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Plane's editions and features:

## Plane Editions Overview

Plane offers three unique self-hosted editions tailored to meet distinct needs—the open-source Community Edition, the recommended Commercial Edition, and the Airgapped Edition.

## Commercial Edition Details

The Commercial Edition remains closed-source to offer enterprise-grade features and seamless scalability for businesses. The Commercial Edition is closed-source and includes a built-in Free tier of 12 user seats per workspace, which means you can run Commercial in production at small scale without buying a license.

## License and Activation

Commercial uses an online license key tied to one workspace and one machine. You pick a plan and purchase your license from the Prime portal, then paste the license key into your commercial instance to sync and unlock every paid feature.

## Prime CLI and Work Items

Prime CLI pulls the commercial edition and starts your instance, running on the free tier until your license is applied. Regarding work items, Community Edition covers core project management, while Commercial adds governance and scale: workflows, approvals, SSO, audit trails, epics, integrations, and air-gapped deployment.

## Community vs. Commercial Feature Availability

Community has parity with the Free tier of Cloud, while Commercial and Airgapped get full parity with Cloud's paid plans (Pro, Business, Enterprise Grid), license-gated.

Based on the provided content, I cannot answer your questions because **the page does not specify which features are Commercial-only versus Community-available**. 

The document only describes the technical migration process for upgrading from Community to Commercial Edition. It mentions "The Commercial edition comes with the free plan and the flexibility to upgrade to a paid plan," but it doesn't list specific feature differences between editions.

The page also doesn't confirm whether Community Edition is the open-source AGPL build, whether Commercial is closed-source, or clarify the relationship to the makeplane/plane GitHub repository.

To find this information, you would need to consult:
- The Plane Editions page (referenced as "/self-hosting/editions-and-versions")
- Feature comparison documentation
- The project's licensing information

# Epics in Plane

**What are Epics?**
Epics are now a work item type in Plane, treated the same as other work item types. They function as a parent layer above individual work items, with all data and relationships preserved from the previous system.

**Hierarchy Structure**
Yes, Epics enable a two-level hierarchy by default. According to the documentation: "Task is created at level 0 and Epic at level 1 - this is the default hierarchy structure." With the Hierarchy feature enabled, Epics sit above Tasks, and Tasks can be sub-items of Epics, but Tasks cannot contain Epics.

**Nesting Depth**
The content indicates a maximum of two hierarchy levels: Epics (level 1) containing Tasks (level 0). The system enforces this structure automatically—deeper nesting isn't mentioned as possible.

**Plan/Edition Gating**
The documentation labels Epics as **"Pro"** at the top of the page, indicating this feature is gated to the Pro plan or higher.

content.text:
I have enough verified, converging evidence across official docs, the GitHub repo, the API reference, SDK repo, and third-party reviews. I've confirmed the critical claims through multiple independent sources. Let me synthesize the final report.

---

# Plane (makeplane/plane) — 2025/2026 Evaluation for a Custom-Data-Model Licensing Company

## Bottom line up front

**No — the company cannot build 100% of its custom functionality on top of *open-source self-hosted Plane* without either (a) paying for the closed-source Commercial Edition or (b) forking.** The single most important finding is that the exact features this workflow depends on — **custom Work Item Types, Custom Properties (typed fields), Workflows, and Epics — are NOT in the open-source AGPL Community Edition.** They live in a **separate, closed-source Commercial Edition** delivered via a proprietary `prime-cli` and unlocked with a license key. Plane is **open-core**, not fully open-source for these capabilities.

And even on the Commercial Edition, the model is *configuration-based*, not *extension-based*: **there is no plugin/extension SDK** for adding custom objects or custom UI. You extend Plane by configuring its built-in types/fields and by building **separate external apps against its REST API + webhooks + OAuth** — not by injecting code into Plane itself.

---

## Editions & licensing (the crux)

- **Community Edition (CE):** Open-source, **AGPL-3.0** (`LICENSE.txt` in repo), self-host via Docker Compose (`setup.sh`) / Kubernetes. Feature parity with the **Free Cloud tier only**. Includes: projects, work items, cycles, modules, pages, 5 view layouts, dashboards, estimates, **REST API, webhooks**, basic custom *states*. No user limits.
- **Commercial Edition:** **Closed-source**, pulled via proprietary **`prime-cli`**, runs free up to 12 seats, then a **license key** (online, tied to one workspace + one machine) unlocks Pro/Business/Enterprise Grid features. There is also an Airgapped Edition.
- **Repo structure:** The public monorepo `apps/` contains `admin, api, live, proxy, space, web` — **there is no `ee`/`enterprise` directory in the public repo.** The Commercial code is *not* in the public GitHub repo; it is delivered as separate proprietary images. So "you can read the EE source and self-build it" is **false** for the gated features.
- The marketing claim that CE has "99.9% parity" and "no hidden code" refers to *core PM*; the governance/data-model features are simply absent from CE, not hidden.

**Plan gating of the features that matter here:**
| Feature | Plan required |
|---|---|
| Custom Properties / typed fields | **Pro** ($7/seat/mo) |
| Work Item Types | **Pro/Business** |
| Epics | **Pro** |
| Workflows (state-transition rules) | **Business** ($17/seat/mo) |
| Approval gates | **Enterprise Grid** |
| SSO (SAML/OIDC/LDAP), audit logs, advanced permissions | **Business/Enterprise** |
| All of the above self-hosted | **Commercial Edition + license key (closed source)** |

---

## Requirement-by-requirement verdict

| # | Requirement | Verdict | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R1** | Multiple custom **object/issue types** (Project, SKU, Collection, Design, Style#) | **Built-in but Commercial-gated** (NOT in AGPL CE) | "Work Item Types" exist; Pro/Business + Commercial Edition only. Not in open-source CE. |
| **R2** | **Typed custom fields** per type | **Built-in but Commercial-gated** | "Custom Properties" feature = Pro plan, Commercial Edition. API exposes property *types, options, values*. Supported types incl. text, number, date, single-select/options, boolean, relation, member (per API `issue-types/properties`). **Not in AGPL CE.** This is the headline answer to your critical question. |
| **R3** | **Multi-level hierarchy ≥3 deep / Epics** | **Partial — Built-in but shallow + gated** | Epics = a work-item type (Pro). Default hierarchy is **2 levels** (Epic L1 → Task L0). Sub-issues exist, but docs describe a 2-level enforced structure; deep arbitrary nesting (sub-issue of sub-issue of sub-issue) is **not** a documented capability. ≥3 typed levels (Collection→Design→Style#) is a **gap/risk**. |
| **R4** | **Custom workflow states** + transition history via API + workflow rules | **States: Built-in (CE). Workflow rules: Commercial-gated. Transition history via API: Buildable-on-top** | Unlimited custom states in CE. **Workflows** (allowed-transition rules, who-can-move, approvals) = Business/Enterprise, Commercial only. The "change history" in Workflows docs tracks *config* changes; per-item state-transition timestamps come from the **activity log** API (compute time-in-stage yourself). |
| **R5** | **Bulk operations via API** | **Built-in (UI) + Buildable-on-top (API)** | Bulk update in List/Table layouts (multi-select → change state/assignee/properties). An internal bulk-update endpoint exists (had an IDOR security advisory GHSA-4q54-h4x9-m329, since patched). For programmatic bulk, loop PATCH per item via REST/SDK. |
| **R6** | **Saved filtered/grouped Views** | **Built-in (CE)** | Views with filters/grouping, shareable saved views — in Community. |
| **R7** | **Field-level RBAC** (hide pricing field from designers) | **Not possible without fork** | Permissions are **feature/action-level only** (Workspace: Owner/Admin/Member/Guest; Project: Admin/Contributor/Commenter/Guest). **No field-/property-level visibility control** exists. A role can edit *all* properties or none. This is a hard blocker for built-in field hiding. |
| **R8** | **Cross-record search/filter** | **Built-in (CE)** | Command-K/Power-K global search; rich filters; API supports search + `fields`/`expand` + filtering. |
| **R9** | **File attachments via S3/R2; thumbnail URLs + filepath links** | **Built-in (CE)** | Attachments/assets API; S3-compatible object storage (works with R2). External thumbnail URLs / filepath links can be stored in description, comments, links, or custom URL/text properties. |
| **R10** | **REST API + webhooks + SDK + app framework** | **Built-in (CE) — strong** | REST API (X-API-Key + **OAuth 2.0** apps, custom scopes), cursor pagination, `fields`/`expand`. Resources: work items, projects, states, labels, **work item types, custom properties + values + options**, cycles, modules, **epics**, **activity logs**, comments, attachments, worklogs, customers, intake, pages. **Official Python SDK** (`makeplane/plane-python-sdk`, Pydantic v2, actively maintained, ~v0.2.x). **No official TS/JS SDK** found. Webhooks for Project/Issue/Cycle/Module/Issue-Comment (HMAC-signed). Rate limit 60 req/min/key. |
| **R11** | **Extensibility WITHOUT forking** (plugin/custom UI/custom backend) | **No plugin system. Build separate apps via API/webhooks/OAuth. Custom UI inside Plane ⇒ fork** | **There is NO frontend plugin SDK and no backend extension/plugin framework.** Confirmed: the only ways to extend are (1) configure built-in types/fields (Commercial), or (2) build an **external app** against REST API + webhooks + OAuth 2.0 and embed/link/SSO. **Any custom UI *inside* the Plane (Next.js) app requires forking the monorepo.** Your stated suspicion is correct. |
| **R12** | **Self-hostable; license; open vs gated** | **Yes; AGPL-3.0 (CE); open-core** | CE = AGPL-3.0, fully self-hostable. Data-model/governance features = closed-source Commercial Edition + license key. |
| **R13** | **UX maturity for non-technical users** | **Risk** | Modern, fast (Linear-like). But reviews note it's **engineering-oriented** (issues/sprints/backlogs terminology), "not designed for non-technical users." A licensing/design team is non-technical → adoption risk. |

---

## Project maturity

- **GitHub:** ~50k+ stars (most-starred OSS PM tool). Monorepo restructured into `apps/` + `packages/`.
- **Releases:** Frequent (roughly monthly + security patches). Note a **versioning discontinuity**: the `v0.x` line (e.g., v0.25.0 Feb 2025, up to ~v0.28) was **rebranded to v1.0.0**, with the latest tag around **v1.3.1**. (Sources gave conflicting 2025-vs-2026 dates for v1.3.1 — treat the exact date as uncertain; the *sequence* CE 0.x → 1.0 rebrand → 1.3.x is solid.)
- **Company/funding:** Founded Nov 2022 by Vamsi & Vihar Kurama (Delaware/India). **$4M seed led by OSS Capital.** ~90+ employees. Repositioning as "AI-native" (Projects + Wiki + AI).
- **Developer platform roadmap:** Real and growing — `developers.plane.so`, OAuth 2.0 apps with custom scopes, official Python SDK, webhooks, expanding API. But **no app marketplace and no plugin/extension SDK** announced.

---

## The single biggest risk / blocker

**The architecture is "configure + integrate," not "extend."** Two compounding problems:

1. **The custom data model you need (types + typed fields + workflows + epics) is open-core gated** — absent from AGPL self-host, available only in the **closed-source Commercial Edition behind a per-seat license** (Pro $7 / Business $17). So "free self-hosted + build on top" does **not** get you custom objects/fields.
2. **No field-level RBAC and no plugin/custom-UI system.** Hiding a "pricing" field from designers (R7) is **not achievable** without forking, and any bespoke UI ("design library" browser, custom pipelines UI) must be built as a **separate external app** against the API — you cannot inject it into Plane's frontend without forking the Next.js monorepo (which AGPL permits but is exactly what you wanted to avoid).

**Net recommendation framing:** Plane *can* serve as a backend-of-record if you (a) buy the Commercial Edition for types/fields/workflows, and (b) build your "design library," role-based field views, and custom pipelines as an **external app on the REST API + webhooks + OAuth + Python SDK**. But the requirements as written — *fully open-source self-host, build 100% on top without forking, with field-level visibility and embedded custom UI* — are **not all simultaneously satisfiable** on Plane today.

---

## Cited URLs

- Editions: https://developers.plane.so/self-hosting/editions-and-versions
- Editions blog (closed-source Commercial, license key): https://plane.so/blog/plane-and-its-editions
- Open-source page (CE vs Commercial feature split): https://plane.so/open-source
- GitHub repo (AGPL, `apps/` structure, no `ee` dir): https://github.com/makeplane/plane and https://github.com/makeplane/plane/tree/master/apps
- License: https://github.com/makeplane/plane/blob/master/LICENSE.txt
- Releases: https://github.com/makeplane/plane/releases
- API reference: https://developers.plane.so/api-reference/introduction
- Work item types / custom properties API: https://developers.plane.so/api-reference/issue-types/types/update-issue-types and .../issue-types/properties/update-property
- Webhooks/OAuth: https://developers.plane.so/dev-tools/intro-webhooks
- Official Python SDK: https://github.com/makeplane/plane-python-sdk
- Workflows (Business/Enterprise gated): https://docs.plane.so/workflows-and-approvals/workflows
- Epics (Pro, 2-level hierarchy): https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/epics
- Bulk ops (UI): https://docs.plane.so/core-concepts/issues/bulk-ops + advisory https://github.com/makeplane/plane/security/advisories/GHSA-4q54-h4x9-m329
- Roles (no field-level): https://docs.plane.so/roles-and-permissions/permissions-matrix and https://docs.plane.so/workspaces-and-users/roles
- Prime CLI (Commercial-only installer): https://developers.plane.so/self-hosting/manage/prime-cli
- Upgrade Community→Commercial: https://developers.plane.so/self-hosting/upgrade-from-community
- Funding: https://plane.so/blog/plane-raises-4m-seed and https://techcrunch.com/2023/11/28/plane-takes-on-jira-with-open-source-project-management-tools-for-software-teams/
- 2025 review (UX, paid features): https://workflowautomation.net/reviews/plane

**Honest gaps:** (1) Exact latest-version *date* is ambiguous across sources (v1.3.1; 2025 vs 2026); the version *sequence* is reliable. (2) The full enumerated list of supported custom-property field *types* is inferred from the API navigation (text/number/date/option/boolean/relation/member) rather than a single quoted matrix — verify on a live Commercial instance before committing. (3) Whether `prime-cli` itself is OSS is not disclosed (it gates closed-source images regardless).
